text.title: Risk Assessment Process and Antipatterns
text.summary: Five-step market-risk assessment process, pre-mortem technique, and AI-agent gotchas.

section.title: Five-Step Process

list.item: Step 1 — Enumerate risks: run the enumeration agent against market-research.md, competitive-analysis.md, pricing-research.md; require minimum 2 risks per sub-category (demand, competition, pricing, trend, channel)
Step 2 — Red-team pre-mortem: run a market-specific pre-mortem with 3 failure branches (segment did not materialize, competitor won, price elasticity broke the model)
Step 3 — Score with citations: apply prob x impact; every cell must cite a specific paragraph or table from the source files; reject rows without citations
Step 4 — Human review: owner accepts or rejects each row and assigns a trigger metric already visible in the company dashboard
Step 5 — Persist and schedule: save as market-risk-register.md; re-run enumeration and pre-mortem passes monthly or on any commit to source research files

section.reference: Risk register template with high/medium/low priority tables and contingency format
Citation-enforcement linter for risk register rows

rule.statement: Run the pre-mortem with 3 distinct agent personas (skeptical customer, well-funded competitor, regulator or platform owner) rather than a single agent pass. Consolidate outputs; do not average them.
rule.rationale: A solo agent run produces a single failure narrative that confirms the briefing document's tone. Multiple adversarial personas surface independent failure paths that the enumeration pass misses.

antipattern.description: Demand risk scored against TAM rather than the validated wedge
antipattern.reason: A large TAM with no validated entry wedge is the highest-loss failure mode; anchoring on TAM produces artificially low demand-risk scores.

antipattern.description: Pricing risk collapsed into one row instead of four sub-rows
antipattern.reason: Aggregating four distinct failure modes (commoditization, price war, anchor mismatch, WTP collapse) into one row averages them and hides the real exposure.

antipattern.description: Channel-dependence risk scored Medium when one channel exceeds 40% of pipeline
antipattern.reason: Any single channel above 40% should be auto-scored High probability unless 3+ years of stable retention evidence exists.

antipattern.description: Risk register updated only at project kick-off
antipattern.reason: Stale registers are worse than none; they create false security. Re-run at minimum monthly and on every commit to source research files.

antipattern.description: Agent quotes multi-billion-dollar TAM and scores demand risk Low
antipattern.reason: Force the prompt to score against the validated wedge (named buyer segment, confirmed problem, pipeline evidence), not the TAM ceiling.

antipattern.description: Agent uses stale competitor lists from training data
antipattern.reason: Require the agent to re-read competitive-analysis.md and verify competitor domains via faion-domain-checker-agent before citing any competitive risk row.

antipattern.description: Agent mitigation language defaults to "diversify channels", "monitor competitors", "iterate pricing"
antipattern.reason: Ban these phrases; require action verb + named owner + measurable trigger + budget cap. Vague mitigations are never executed.

antipattern.description: Agent ships "Avoid" response on market risk autonomously (e.g., killing a segment)
antipattern.reason: Avoid-strategy responses mean killing a segment, geo, or pricing tier — never let an agent execute this unattended. Hard human-in-the-loop gate required.

antipattern.description: Risk IDs change every agent run (R1..Rn renumbered)
antipattern.reason: Use a stable hash of the risk statement or instruct the agent to diff-merge against the existing register; changing IDs break longitudinal tracking.
